slides.title: Unit 4
slides.subtitle: MS PowerPoint
slides.notes: Teach Unit 4 as a modular block (6 hours).

slides.title: Unit 4 — MS PowerPoint for Legal Presentations
slides.notes: Six hours, 10% weight. Advocate clarity over decoration. One idea per slide. Speaker notes support moot delivery.

slides.title: PowerPoint in Legal and Academic Context
bullets: Communicate case facts, arguments, and awareness topics
Used in moot courts, seminars, and client meetings
Slides support oral advocacy — you are the presenter
Poor slides distract judges and examiners
Lab: moot court deck and legal awareness animation
8–12 slides is a typical student presentation length
slides.notes: Set design philosophy early: slides are visual aids, not scripts. Moot and seminar contexts have different pacing needs.

slides.title: Slide Design Principles
bullets: One main idea per slide — avoid walls of text
Large readable fonts; high contrast background
Limit bullets to five or six per slide
Use images, diagrams, or quotes — not paragraphs
Consistent alignment and spacing throughout
Speaker notes hold detail; slides stay minimal
slides.notes: Show a bad dense slide versus a clean one. Contrast and font size matter in courtrooms with projectors.

slides.title: Themes and Templates
bullets: Themes apply coordinated colors, fonts, and effects
Templates provide pre-built slide layouts
Choose professional themes — avoid distracting backgrounds
Slide Master edits fonts and placeholders globally
Match theme to context: formal moot vs awareness campaign
campus-blue and academic themes suit law presentations
slides.notes: Demo applying a theme and editing Slide Master for consistent footers. Formal moot decks need restrained visual design.

slides.title: Animations vs Transitions

left.heading: Transitions
bullets: Effect when moving between entire slides
Examples: Fade, Push, Wipe
Use subtle transitions — one style for whole deck
Overuse distracts judges and examiners

right.heading: Animations
bullets: Effects on objects within a single slide
Reveal bullet points or diagram steps sequentially
Build complex arguments step by step
Purpose: direct attention, not entertainment

slides.notes: Exam distinction: transitions = between slides; animations = within a slide. Both should be purposeful and minimal.

slides.title: SmartArt and Charts in Slides
bullets: SmartArt visualizes processes, hierarchies, and cycles
Use for legal procedures or organizational structures
Insert Chart links to Excel data for live updates
Prefer SmartArt over bullet walls for relationships
Keep diagrams simple — label every element clearly
Test readability from the back of the room
slides.notes: When to use SmartArt vs bullets is a common exam point. Show a simple litigation process or court hierarchy diagram.

slides.title: Legal Case Presentations
bullets: Open with caption: parties, court, statute involved
Facts slide — chronological, neutral tone
Issues framed as precise legal questions
Arguments split: petitioner vs respondent points
Relief or prayer stated clearly on dedicated slide
Annexures referenced; do not crowd slides with citations
slides.notes: Case presentation structure mirrors written pleadings. Slides summarize; oral argument adds authority and analysis.

slides.title: Moot Court Presentation Structure
bullets: Facts → Issues → Arguments → Prayer — classic flow
Allocate time: facts brief, arguments longest
Anticipate judge interruptions — backup slides optional
Speaker notes contain citations and case names
Closing slide: precise relief sought
Rehearse with timer; cut excess animation
slides.notes: Moot deck is a lab deliverable. Emphasize speaker notes for citations while slides stay clean. Facts–issues–arguments–prayer is exam-ready structure.

slides.title: Seminar and Legal Awareness Decks
bullets: Hook slide: striking question or statistic
Define key legal terms for general audience
Use examples and scenarios — not jargon alone
One awareness message per section
Call to action: rights, remedies, or helplines
Credits slide: sources and team members
slides.notes: Seminar decks educate non-lawyers. Contrast with moot formality. Legal awareness lab may use light animation for emphasis.

slides.title: Unit 4 — Exam Tips
bullets: State design principles: one idea, contrast, readability
Distinguish animation (within slide) vs transition (between)
When to use SmartArt instead of bullet lists
Outline moot deck structure: facts–issues–arguments–prayer
Pitfall: wall-of-text slides and excessive effects
Pitfall: unreadable color combinations
slides.notes: Design principles and moot structure are high-yield. Review a sample deck critically before the practical exam.

slides.title: Unit 4 — Next steps
bullets: Complete unit labs
Attempt unit Q&A bank
Submit unit assignment
Revise using revision sheet
slides.notes: Point students to materials/labs and materials/qa for this unit.
